{

      "id": 1,
      "title": "Mathematical Foundations: Logic, Sets, and Proofs",
      "estimated_minutes": 240,
      "description": "Reawakening your high school math intuition. Before writing any code, we must establish the formal language used to prove an algorithm actually works. We'll start from first principles with logic, set theory, and mathematical induction.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Construct formal proofs using mathematical induction and proof by contradiction, and manipulate summations and set operations.",
        "concepts": [
          "Sets, Relations, and Functions",

      "title": "Mathematical Foundations: Combinatorics and Probability",
      "estimated_minutes": 240,
      "description": "Algorithms often deal with permutations of data and randomized choices. This module rebuilds your understanding of counting, discrete probability, and random variables to prepare you for analyzing average-case algorithmic performance.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Calculate probabilities, expected values, and permutations, laying the groundwork for analyzing randomized algorithms.",
        "concepts": [

      "title": "First Principles of Algorithms: Insertion Sort and Loop Invariants",
      "estimated_minutes": 180,
      "description": "Your first foray into the core of CLRS. We will define what an algorithm is, learn how to read the book's pseudocode, implement Insertion Sort in Python, and rigorously prove its correctness using Loop Invariants.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Translate CLRS pseudocode into working code, trace memory state, and formally prove algorithm correctness using loop invariants.",

      "estimated_minutes": 210,
      "description": "How do we talk about the speed of an algorithm without relying on hardware specs? We use asymptotic notation. We will rigorously define Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta using the formal mathematics established in Module 1.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Formally prove the asymptotic bounds of given functions using Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta definitions.",

      "estimated_minutes": 240,

      "estimated_minutes": 240,

      "estimated_minutes": 240,
      "description": "Quicksort is practically the most important sorting algorithm. We will dissect its partitioning logic, understand why its worst case is terrible, and rigorously prove why its expected case is incredibly fast using probability.",

parsed.goal: Comprehensive mastery from scratch; seeking an interactive, first-principles alternative to traditional reading.

modules.title: Mathematical Foundations: Logic, Sets, and Proofs
modules.description: Reawakening your high school math intuition. Before writing any code, we must establish the formal language used to prove an algorithm actually works. We'll start from first principles with logic, set theory, and mathematical induction.

plan.goal: Construct formal proofs using mathematical induction and proof by contradiction, and manipulate summations and set operations.

modules.title: Mathematical Foundations: Combinatorics and Probability
modules.description: Algorithms often deal with permutations of data and randomized choices. This module rebuilds your understanding of counting, discrete probability, and random variables to prepare you for analyzing average-case algorithmic performance.

plan.goal: Calculate probabilities, expected values, and permutations, laying the groundwork for analyzing randomized algorithms.

modules.title: First Principles of Algorithms: Insertion Sort and Loop Invariants
modules.description: Your first foray into the core of CLRS. We will define what an algorithm is, learn how to read the book's pseudocode, implement Insertion Sort in Python, and rigorously prove its correctness using Loop Invariants.

plan.goal: Translate CLRS pseudocode into working code, trace memory state, and formally prove algorithm correctness using loop invariants.
concepts: The Role of Algorithms in Computing

modules.title: The Language of Time: Asymptotic Notation
modules.description: How do we talk about the speed of an algorithm without relying on hardware specs? We use asymptotic notation. We will rigorously define Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta using the formal mathematics established in Module 1.

plan.goal: Formally prove the asymptotic bounds of given functions using Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta definitions.

modules.title: Divide-and-Conquer: Merge Sort and Recurrence Relations
modules.description: Introducing a major paradigm: breaking problems into smaller subproblems. You will implement Merge Sort and learn the three primary methods for solving the mathematical recurrence equations that describe divide-and-conquer runtimes.

plan.goal: Implement Merge Sort and solve complex recurrence equations using the Substitution Method, Recursion Trees, and the Master Theorem.

modules.title: Probabilistic Analysis and Randomized Algorithms
modules.description: Applying your Module 2 math skills to algorithms. We'll study the 'Hiring Problem' to understand how randomizing the input or the algorithm's choices can drastically improve expected running times.

plan.goal: Analyze the expected running time of randomized algorithms using indicator random variables.

modules.title: Sorting I: Heaps and Priority Queues
modules.description: Your first advanced data structure. We will view an array as a nearly complete binary tree to build a Max-Heap. From there, we will design Heapsort and a Priority Queue, bridging data structure design with sorting.

plan.goal: Implement a Max-Heap, prove its properties, and build both Heapsort and a dynamic Priority Queue from scratch.
sourceRefs: Part II Sorting and Order Statistics (pp. 157-248)

modules.title: Sorting II: Quicksort and Rigorous Expected-Time Analysis
modules.description: Quicksort is practically the most important sorting algorithm. We will dissect its partitioning logic, understand why its worst case is terrible, and rigorously prove why its expected case is incredibly fast using probability.

plan.goal: Implement randomized Quicksort and mathematically prove its O(n log n) expected runtime.
concepts: Rigorous Analysis of Quicksort using Expected Value
sourceRefs: Part II Sorting and Order Statistics (pp. 157-248)

modules.title: Sorting in Linear Time: Breaking the Comparison Barrier
modules.description: Can we sort faster than O(n log n)? Yes, if we don't compare elements. We will explore the theoretical lower bounds of comparison sorting, then learn Counting Sort, Radix Sort, and Bucket Sort.

plan.goal: Explain the decision-tree lower bound for sorting and implement non-comparison linear-time sorting algorithms.
sourceRefs: Part II Sorting and Order Statistics (pp. 157-248)

modules.title: Medians and Order Statistics
modules.description: Finding the median or the 'i-th' smallest element in an unsorted array without fully sorting it. We'll explore a randomized algorithm and the famous deterministic linear-time selection algorithm.

plan.goal: Implement randomized and deterministic linear-time selection algorithms to find the i-th order statistic.
concepts: Selection in Worst-Case Linear Time (Median of Medians)
sourceRefs: Part II Sorting and Order Statistics (pp. 157-248)

modules.title: Elementary Data Structures: Memory Under the Hood
modules.description: Before mastering complex trees, we must understand how dynamic sets are stored in memory. We will build Stacks, Queues, Linked Lists, and tree representations from scratch, dealing directly with pointers (or array indices).

plan.goal: Implement stacks, queues, and doubly linked lists, and map abstract tree structures into physical array/pointer memory models.
prerequisites: The Role of Algorithms in Computing

modules.title: Hash Tables and Universal Hashing
modules.description: Hash tables offer O(1) average-time operations. We will dive deep into hash functions, resolve collisions using chaining and open addressing, and mathematically analyze Universal Hashing.

plan.goal: Implement a hash table with collision resolution and prove the expected constant-time operations using universal hashing.

modules.title: Binary Search Trees
modules.description: The foundation of dynamic ordered data. We will write traversal, search, insertion, and deletion algorithms for BSTs, and analyze how tree height dictates performance.

plan.goal: Implement all primary operations of a Binary Search Tree and analyze the impact of node insertion order on tree height.
concepts: Querying a BST (Search, Minimum, Maximum, Successor, Predecessor)
Expected Height of a Randomly Built BST

modules.title: Red-Black Trees: Guaranteed Balance
modules.description: A notoriously challenging and rewarding chapter. We will rigorously define Red-Black properties, implement tree rotations, and write the complex, multi-case logic to maintain perfect O(log n) balance during insertions and deletions.

plan.goal: Implement a fully functional Red-Black Tree, mastering the rotation and color-flipping cases for insertion and deletion.

modules.title: Advanced Design I: Dynamic Programming Foundations
modules.description: Moving to Part IV, we tackle one of the most powerful paradigms: Dynamic Programming. We will learn to spot overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure, using Rod Cutting as our primary vehicle.

plan.goal: Identify optimal substructure in a problem and implement both top-down memoized and bottom-up dynamic programming solutions.
concepts: Elements of Dynamic Programming (Optimal Substructure, Overlapping Subproblems)
sourceRefs: Part IV Advanced Design and Analysis Techniques (pp. 361-476)

modules.title: Advanced Design II: Complex Dynamic Programming
modules.description: Applying DP to harder problems. We will trace 2D memory tables for Matrix-Chain Multiplication and the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) algorithms.

plan.goal: Design and implement 2D dynamic programming algorithms for sequences and optimization tasks.
sourceRefs: Part IV Advanced Design and Analysis Techniques (pp. 361-476)

modules.title: Advanced Design III: Greedy Algorithms
modules.description: Sometimes, choosing the local best option leads to the global optimal solution. We will prove the greedy-choice property using the Activity Selection problem and implement Huffman Codes.

plan.goal: Prove a problem has the greedy-choice property and implement greedy algorithms like Huffman Coding.
sourceRefs: Part IV Advanced Design and Analysis Techniques (pp. 361-476)

modules.title: Amortized Analysis
modules.description: How do we analyze algorithms where occasional operations are expensive, but most are cheap? We will master the Aggregate, Accounting, and Potential methods to prove average performance over a sequence of operations.

plan.goal: Perform amortized analysis on dynamic arrays using the aggregate, accounting, and potential methods.
sourceRefs: Part IV Advanced Design and Analysis Techniques (pp. 361-476)

modules.title: Advanced Data Structures I: B-Trees
modules.description: When data is too large to fit in RAM, we must design algorithms for magnetic disks or SSDs. B-Trees generalize binary trees to massive scale. We will study disk models and implement node splitting.

plan.goal: Explain secondary storage models and implement B-Tree insertion and splitting.
concepts: Memory Hierarchies and Disk Access Models
Deleting a Key from a B-Tree

modules.title: Advanced Data Structures II: Disjoint Sets
modules.description: An incredibly efficient structure for tracking connected components. We will implement Union-Find with path compression and union-by-rank, observing an amortized runtime so slow-growing it is practically constant.

plan.goal: Implement Disjoint-Set forests and mathematically analyze the extreme efficiency of path compression.
concepts: Union by Rank and Path Compression
Analysis using the Inverse Ackermann Function (Overview)

modules.title: Graph Algorithms I: Representation and Search
modules.description: Part VI begins. Graphs map relationships between data. We will represent graphs in memory, implement Breadth-First and Depth-First Search, and extract metadata like Topological Sortings.

plan.goal: Represent graphs programmatically and implement BFS, DFS, and Topological Sort to analyze network structures.

modules.title: Graph Algorithms II: Minimum Spanning Trees
modules.description: Connecting a network with the least possible wire. We will leverage our disjoint sets and priority queues to build and formally prove Kruskal's and Prim's Greedy algorithms.

plan.goal: Implement Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms and formally prove they yield Minimum Spanning Trees using the Cut Property.
concepts: Growing a Minimum Spanning Tree (The Cut Property)

modules.title: Graph Algorithms III: Single-Source Shortest Paths
modules.description: How mapping apps find routes. We will rigorously explore edge relaxation, Dijkstra's algorithm for positive weights, and the Bellman-Ford algorithm to handle negative weights.

plan.goal: Implement Dijkstra's and Bellman-Ford algorithms, proving path correctness via edge relaxation properties.
concepts: Properties of Shortest Paths and Relaxation
Single-Source Shortest Paths in Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)

modules.title: Graph Algorithms IV: All-Pairs Shortest Paths
modules.description: Finding paths between every pair of nodes simultaneously. We will connect this graph problem back to Dynamic Programming and Matrix Multiplication, learning the elegant Floyd-Warshall algorithm.

plan.goal: Use dynamic programming to solve the all-pairs shortest paths problem via the Floyd-Warshall and Johnson's algorithms.
concepts: Transitive Closure of a Directed Graph

modules.title: Graph Algorithms V: Maximum Flow
modules.description: Modeling fluid through pipes, data through networks, or bipartite matching. We will master the concept of residual networks and prove the beautiful Max-flow Min-cut theorem.

plan.goal: Implement the Ford-Fulkerson method and prove network capacities using the Max-flow Min-cut theorem.
concepts: The Ford-Fulkerson Method (Residual networks, Augmenting paths)

modules.title: Selected Topics: Multithreaded Algorithms and Matrices
modules.description: Modern hardware relies on concurrency. We will learn the theoretic underpinnings of dynamic multithreading, compute span and work, and apply these to multithreaded matrix operations.

plan.goal: Analyze the speedup and span of multithreaded algorithms, particularly matrix multiplication and merge sort.

modules.title: Selected Topics: Linear Programming
modules.description: Linear programming generalizes many optimization problems (like max flow). We will formulate problems as linear programs and explore the conceptual mechanics of the Simplex algorithm.

plan.goal: Formulate algorithmic problems as standard and slack linear programs, and understand geometric feasibility.

modules.title: Selected Topics: String Matching
modules.description: How search functions work in text. We will start with naive approaches, use hashing in the Rabin-Karp algorithm, and study the elegant state-machine logic of the Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm.

plan.goal: Implement Rabin-Karp and KMP algorithms for efficient O(n) string searching.

modules.title: The Edge of Feasibility: NP-Completeness
modules.description: Some problems might be impossible to solve quickly. We will define the classes P and NP, understand what makes a problem NP-Complete, and write mathematical reductions to prove hardness.

plan.goal: Formally distinguish between P and NP complexity classes and execute polynomial-time reductions to prove NP-Completeness.
concepts: Polynomial Time and the class P
Polynomial-Time Verification and the class NP
Classic NP-Complete Problems (3-CNF-SAT, Clique, Vertex Cover, Subset-Sum)

modules.title: Coping with Hardness: Approximation Algorithms
modules.description: When a problem is NP-Complete, we must compromise. We conclude the CLRS journey by designing algorithms that guarantee a solution within a mathematical ratio of the perfect answer.

plan.goal: Design and mathematically analyze algorithms that provide guaranteed approximation bounds for NP-hard problems.
concepts: Randomization and Linear Programming in Approximation
